text:
The TLS connection chain in Firefox works as follows:

**Hop 1 — netwerk/protocol/http/nsHttpConnection.cpp + TlsHandshaker.cpp**: `nsHttpConnection::Activate()` (line ~587) calls `mTlsHandshaker->SetupSSL()`. The `TlsHandshaker::SetupSSL()` method (TlsHandshaker.cpp:72) initiates SSL setup on the socket transport, configuring NPN/ALPN negotiation and transitioning the connection state to TLS_HANDSHAKING.

**Hop 2 — security/manager/ssl/nsNSSIOLayer.h (class) + nsNSSIOLayer.cpp (implementation)**: The `nsSSLIOLayerHelpers` class (nsNSSIOLayer.h:38) bridges Necko to NSS. It manages TLS version intolerance info and fallback sites. The key entry points are `nsSSLIOLayerNewSocket()` (nsNSSIOLayer.cpp:1247) which creates a TCP socket and calls `nsSSLIOLayerAddToSocket()` (line 1790), and `nsSSLIOLayerImportFD()` (line 1269) which calls `SSL_ImportFD()` to push the NSS SSL layer onto the NSPR file descriptor stack and registers callbacks (HandshakeCallback, SecretCallback, CanFalseStartCallback).

**Hop 3 — security/nss/lib/ssl/sslsecur.c**: `ssl_SecureConnect()` (line 692) sets `ss->handshake = ssl_BeginClientHandshake` and initiates the TCP connect. `ssl_Do1stHandshake()` (line 33) is the main handshake driver loop — called from `SSL_ForceHandshake()` (line 383) and the secure send/recv paths — it repeatedly invokes `ss->handshake()` until the handshake completes.

**Hop 4 — security/nss/lib/ssl/ssl3con.c**: `ssl3_SendClientHello()` (line 5505) constructs and sends the TLS ClientHello message, including version negotiation, session resumption, cipher suites, and TLS extensions. This is the actual start of the TLS handshake protocol on the wire.
